title: Duty Cycle Limits and Weld Setup Procedure
summary: This section explains the duty cycle ratings for TIG and Stick welding at 120VAC and 240VAC, including thermal protection behavior. It also covers how to prepare workpieces and set up for welding, including surface cleaning, clamping, and ground cable placement.
key_facts: Duty cycle defines minutes within a 10-minute period a welder can weld without overheating.
TIG at 240VAC: 30% duty cycle at 175A (3 minutes welding, 7 minutes rest).
TIG at 120VAC: 40% duty cycle at 125A (4 minutes welding, 6 minutes rest).
Stick at 240VAC: 25% duty cycle at 175A (2.5 minutes welding, 7.5 minutes rest).
Stick at 120VAC: 40% duty cycle at 80A (4 minutes welding, 6 minutes rest).
TIG at 240VAC supports 100% continuous use at 105A.
TIG at 120VAC supports 100% continuous use at 90A.
Stick at 240VAC supports 100% continuous use at 100A.
Stick at 120VAC supports 100% continuous use at 60A.
Overheating triggers automatic shutdown and a warning screen on the LCD Display.
The welder automatically returns to service after cooling off.
Weld surfaces must be free of rust, paint, oil, and other materials — only bare metal.
procedure_steps: 1. Make practice welds on scrap of the same thickness as the intended workpiece. Clean weld surfaces thoroughly with a wire brush or angle grinder to bare metal — no rust, paint, oil, or other materials allowed.

2. Use clamps (not included) to hold workpieces in position. Control the gap between workpieces to allow the weld to hold both sides securely and penetrate fully. Chamfer (bevel) the edges of thicker workpieces to allow proper weld penetration.
3. Clamp the ground cable to bare metal on the workpiece near the weld area, or to a metal work bench where the workpiece is clamped. Ground connection placement depends on desired welding polarity.

NOTICE: When welding equipment on a vehicle, disconnect the vehicle battery from both the positive connection and the ground before welding to prevent damage to vehicle electrical systems and electronics.
warnings: Failure to carefully observe duty cycle limitations can easily over-stress a welder's power generation system contributing to premature welder failure.
Should this occur, rest the Tig Torch or Electrode Holder on an electrically non-conductive, heat-proof surface, such as a concrete slab, well clear of the ground clamp.
Allow the Welder to cool with the power Switch on, so that the internal Fan will help cool the Welder.
When normal operation resumes, use shorter welding periods and longer rest periods to prevent needless wear.

When welding equipment on a vehicle, disconnect the vehicle battery power from both the positive connection and the ground before welding. This prevents damage to some vehicle electrical systems and electronics due to the high voltage and high frequency bursts common in welding.
